medications.indication_rationale: Short course for pain/inflammation; source advised 400mg up to three times daily with food for 5–7 days.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 400mg orally up to three times daily with food for 5–7 days (as per source note)
1000mg orally up to four times daily (max 4g/day) (as per source note)

medications.indication_rationale: Not a first-line or telehealth intervention; reserved for refractory cases under specialist care.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Do plantar-fascia and calf stretches (including a pre-first-step towel stretch before standing) and roll a frozen bottle under the foot; wear supportive cushioned shoes indoors and avoid barefoot walking on hard floors.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Stretching pre-loads the fascia and reduces microtrauma; support offloads the heel.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Modify aggravating load/standing time while symptoms settle.

patient_education_points.point: This is plantar fasciitis — the tissue under your heel is overloaded and inflamed where it attaches to the heel bone; overnight it tightens, so the first steps re-stretch it and hurt. It's benign but can take a while (weeks to months). An X-ray isn't needed.

Before your first steps, loop a towel around your foot and pull your toes toward you to pre-stretch; wear supportive shoes (no barefoot on hard floors), stretch your calf, and roll a frozen bottle under the foot in the evening.

See a doctor if the pain becomes a constant dull ache at night/rest, or squeezing the sides of your heel becomes painful — that could suggest a stress fracture needing imaging. Go to ED if your foot becomes numb, cold or pale, or you suddenly can't push off (possible tendon rupture).

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Progress; podiatry/physiotherapy for biomechanical assessment/orthotics if persistent.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Night/rest pain or positive calcaneal squeeze — review/imaging; numb/cold foot or loss of push-off — ED.

must_include_items: Recognise plantar fasciitis (first-step pattern) and withhold acute imaging
Plantar-fascia/calf stretching + supportive footwear + activity modification
acceptable_alternatives: NSAID and/or paracetamol; heel cushioning/orthoses; podiatry/physio referral
errors_of_omission: Not applying the pattern / no stress-fracture safety-net
errors_of_commission: Ordering acute X-ray for classic plantar fasciitis
Recommending a corticosteroid injection as first-line remotely
Advising continued barefoot walking on hard floors
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: Pass requires recognising plantar fasciitis, conservative stretching/footwear management with no acute imaging, and a stress-fracture safety-net.
